content: {"answer": "The search results include two published real-world IF app studies (Torres 2022 and Huntriss 2024 on SIMPLE) plus marketing and review material. The Torres 2022 study (Sources 1, 2, 4, 11), a 52-week observational study of two commercial IF mobile apps, reported that by week 26 users lost over 1% of starting weight per Eligible Fasting Hour per day on average (Source 4). Huntriss 2024 (Sources 3, 12) found that SIMPLE app engagement was directly associated with weight loss, and Valinskas 2023 (Source 13) reported that mobile apps can act as platforms improving adherence and motivation for IF-based dietary interventions. A real-world healthcare-setting TRE study (Source 7) reported 'modest but significant reductions in weight, BMI, and waist circumference' when TRE was advised in clinic, aligning with prior RCT magnitudes (the same source). Marketing material (Sources 5, 6, 8) claims Zero users improve weight and metabolic markers after 3 months, and Source 9 cites Medium-published anecdotes of 30-pound and 50-100-pound losses, but these are not peer-reviewed effect sizes. The sources do NOT provide direct head-to-head comparisons of real-world app effect sizes versus peer-reviewed RCT effect sizes, nor do they report dropout curves or retention quartiles in a standardized way. Marketing claims generally reference ANY weight loss and metabolic improvement without disclosing attrition-adjusted effect sizes. A CTG registered pilot (Source 14) for app-assisted TRF in hypertensive/obese patients exists but no results surfaced. Missing information: direct effect-size comparison between app-user cohorts and RCT arms, standardized 12-24 week dropout curves, and per-user cost or revenue data.", "key_findings": ["Torres 2022 (n reported in source): by week 26, users of two IF apps lost >1% of starting weight per Eligible Fasting Hour per day on average (Source 4).", "Huntriss 2024: SIMPLE app engagement is directly associated with weight loss in adults with overweight/obesity (Sources 3, 12).", "Real-world clinical TRE (Source 7): modest but significant reductions in weight, BMI, and waist circumference, consistent with controlled-trial magnitudes.", "Marketing claims (Sources 5, 9) cite large individual-level losses not representative of average app-user outcomes.", "No sources provide direct head-to-head effect-size comparisons between app-user cohorts and peer-reviewed RCT arms, or standardized dropout/retention metrics."], "sources_used": ["https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9579929/", "https://mhealth.jmir.org/2022/10/e35896", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11091450/", "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/362290711_Retention_Fasting_Patterns_and_Weight_Loss_With_an_Intermittent_Fasting_App_A_Large-scale_52-week_Observational_Study_Preprint", "https://zerolongevity.com/blog/your-first-3-months-with-zero-what-to-expect/", "https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/16/24/4426", "https://medium.com/@ed_55112/is-zero-fasting-app-worth-70-the-2025-reality-check-no-ones-talking-about-35145cb991c6", "https://www.sciencedirect.com/org/science/article/pii/S2561326X23007047"]}
